metadata.title: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention

one_liner.achieved: near-zero KV cache waste and flexible sharing across requests
one_liner.via: PagedAttention algorithm and vLLM serving system with logical-to-physical block mapping
one_liner.because: virtual memory paging principles eliminate fragmentation and enable dynamic memory allocation for variable-length sequences

claim_tree.statement: Existing LLM serving systems waste KV cache memory due to fragmentation and redundant duplication, limiting batch size.

children.statement: The KV Cache size grows quickly with the number of requests; for a 13B OPT model, a single token requires 800 KB, and a full 2048‑token request can consume up to 1.6 GB of GPU memory.

Complex decoding algorithms, such as random sampling and beam search, require varying KV cache sharing strategies; for example, the prompt part can be shared to minimize memory usage, while the autoregressive generation phase must keep KV caches unshared.

Requests exhibit variability in input and output lengths, requiring the memory management system to accommodate a wide range of prompt lengths and to make scheduling decisions such as deleting or swapping out KV caches.
PagedAttention, an attention algorithm inspired by virtual memory paging, is proposed.

claim.statement: PagedAttention, an attention algorithm inspired by virtual memory paging, is proposed.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: state-of-the-art LLM serving systems such as FasterTransformer and Orca

children.statement: PagedAttention uses a fused GPU kernel that reads KV cache blocks according to the block table and computes attention on the fly, assigning one warp per block to ensure coalesced memory access and supporting variable sequence lengths within a batch.

claim.statement: PagedAttention uses a fused GPU kernel that reads KV cache blocks according to the block table and computes attention on the fly, assigning one warp per block to ensure coalesced memory access and supporting variable sequence lengths within a batch.
entity_names: Fused block read and attention kernel

systems_facets.s4_mapping: KV cache block -> GPU memory block via block table
systems_facets.s5_policy: Assign one warp per KV block

children.statement: Block size 16 is large enough to efficiently utilize the GPU and small enough to avoid significant internal fragmentation in most workloads.
vLLM, an LLM serving system built on PagedAttention, achieves near‑zero KV cache waste and flexible sharing within and across requests.

claim.statement: vLLM, an LLM serving system built on PagedAttention, achieves near‑zero KV cache waste and flexible sharing within and across requests.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: KV cache memory usage vs batch throughput

algorithm_facets.g3: Near-zero KV cache waste and flexible sharing across requests

children.statement: vLLM allows logical blocks of different requests to be mapped to non-contiguous physical blocks, enabling efficient utilization of GPU memory across requests.

claim.statement: vLLM allows logical blocks of different requests to be mapped to non-contiguous physical blocks, enabling efficient utilization of GPU memory across requests.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV block -> physical KV block via block table

children.statement: vLLM maps logical KV blocks to physical KV blocks, storing the KV cache of tokens in logical blocks and allocating new physical blocks as needed during autoregressive decoding.

claim.statement: vLLM maps logical KV blocks to physical KV blocks, storing the KV cache of tokens in logical blocks and allocating new physical blocks as needed during autoregressive decoding.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: pre-allocating KV cache for maximum sequence length
universal_facets.core_tradeoff: larger block size boosts parallelism but increases fragmentation

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV block -> physical KV block via block table
systems_facets.s5_policy: Allocate new physical block when logical block is full during decoding

children.statement: Storing multiple tokens within a KV block (block size > 1) enables the PagedAttention kernel to process the KV cache across more positions in parallel, increasing hardware utilization and reducing latency.

claim.statement: Storing multiple tokens within a KV block (block size > 1) enables the PagedAttention kernel to process the KV cache across more positions in parallel, increasing hardware utilization and reducing latency.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: PagedAttention kernel processing of KV cache

representation_facets.p1: Tokens of a language model sequence
representation_facets.p3: Parallel processing of KV cache positions
representation_facets.p4: Grouping tokens into larger KV blocks

children.statement: 20–26% higher attention kernel latency compared to FasterTransformer.
The overhead of recomputation remains constant across different block sizes.
Recomputation is more efficient when the block size is small.
Swapping is more efficient when the block size is large.
vLLM uses the PagedAttention kernel to access previous KV cache stored in logical blocks and to save newly generated KV cache into physical blocks.

claim.statement: vLLM uses the PagedAttention kernel to access previous KV cache stored in logical blocks and to save newly generated KV cache into physical blocks.

algorithm_facets.g5: KV cache in logical and physical blocks

children.statement: vLLM dynamically assigns new physical blocks to logical blocks as more tokens are generated, filling blocks left to right and allocating a new physical block only when all previous blocks are full, thereby limiting memory waste within a request to one block.
For OPT-13B on the ShareGPT dataset, vLLM processes 2.2× more requests at the same time than Orca (Oracle) and 4.3× more requests than Orca (Max).
Once a request finishes generation, its KV blocks can be freed and reused to store KV cache of other requests.

claim.statement: Once a request finishes generation, its KV blocks can be freed and reused to store KV cache of other requests.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV block -> physical KV block via block table

children.statement: vLLM does not reserve memory for the maximum possible generated sequence length initially; instead, it reserves only the necessary KV blocks to accommodate the KV cache generated during prompt computation.

claim.statement: vLLM does not reserve memory for the maximum possible generated sequence length initially; instead, it reserves only the necessary KV blocks to accommodate the KV cache generated during prompt computation.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV block -> physical KV block via block table
systems_facets.s5_policy: allocate new physical block when logical block full, left-to-right allocation
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with memory and PagedAttention kernel support

children.statement: Compaction is impractical in performance-sensitive LLM serving systems due to the massive KV cache.
children.rejected_why: impractical due to massive KV cache

children.statement: Pre-allocated chunk space for each request prevents memory sharing specific to decoding algorithms in existing memory management systems.
children.rejected_why: prevents memory sharing specific to decoding algorithms

children.statement: Virtual memory and paging is effective for managing the KV cache in LLM serving because the workload requires dynamic memory allocation and is GPU memory bound.
The recomputation method to recover evicted blocks is not feasible in OS.
vLLM implements an all‑or‑nothing eviction policy that evicts either all or none of the blocks of a sequence, since all blocks of a sequence are accessed together.

claim.statement: vLLM implements an all‑or‑nothing eviction policy that evicts either all or none of the blocks of a sequence, since all blocks of a sequence are accessed together.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: sequence -> GPU KV cache blocks via all-or-nothing eviction

children.statement: vLLM uses a first‑come‑first‑serve scheduling policy for all requests and a preemption policy that serves the earliest arrived requests first while preempting the latest requests first, ensuring fairness and preventing starvation.

claim.statement: vLLM uses a first‑come‑first‑serve scheduling policy for all requests and a preemption policy that serves the earliest arrived requests first while preempting the latest requests first, ensuring fairness and preventing starvation.

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory used for KV cache
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Request -> GPU KV cache blocks via all-or-nothing eviction
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with memory and CPU RAM for swapping

children.statement: vLLM gang‑schedules multiple sequences within a single request as a sequence group, preempting or rescheduling them together to preserve potential memory sharing across those sequences.

claim.statement: vLLM gang‑schedules multiple sequences within a single request as a sequence group, preempting or rescheduling them together to preserve potential memory sharing across those sequences.

algorithm_facets.g2: Preemption and rescheduling of sequence groups

children.statement: vLLM implements a swapping mechanism that transfers evicted KV cache blocks to CPU RAM via a CPU block allocator, bounded by the GPU memory allocated for the KV cache, and pauses new requests until preempted sequences finish.

claim.statement: vLLM implements a swapping mechanism that transfers evicted KV cache blocks to CPU RAM via a CPU block allocator, bounded by the GPU memory allocated for the KV cache, and pauses new requests until preempted sequences finish.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: evicted KV cache blocks -> CPU RAM blocks via CPU block allocator
systems_facets.s5_policy: All-or-nothing eviction of entire sequence blocks
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU memory and CPU RAM with bandwidth between them

children.statement: vLLM recomputes KV cache for preempted sequences upon rescheduling, leveraging concatenated prompts to reduce recomputation latency.
In the ShareGPT trace, block sizes from 16 to 128 tokens lead to the best performance.
Swapping incurs excessive overhead with small block sizes.
Recomputation overhead is never higher than 20% of swapping’s latency.
For medium block sizes from 16 to 64, the two methods exhibit comparable end-to-end performance.
vLLM’s all-or-nothing swap-out policy exploits the fact that processing a request requires all of its corresponding token states to be stored in GPU memory.
The system design of vLLM operates in a distributed setting, coordinating multiple GPU workers via the centralized scheduler.

claim.statement: The system design of vLLM operates in a distributed setting, coordinating multiple GPU workers via the centralized scheduler.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV cache -> physical GPU memory via scheduler instructions

children.statement: The KV cache manager in vLLM manages the KV cache in a paged fashion, enabled by PagedAttention, by controlling physical KV cache memory on GPU workers through instructions from the centralized scheduler.

claim.statement: The KV cache manager in vLLM manages the KV cache in a paged fashion, enabled by PagedAttention, by controlling physical KV cache memory on GPU workers through instructions from the centralized scheduler.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: UNSPECIFIED

children.statement: vLLM supports distributed execution of large language models by integrating Megatron-LM style tensor model parallelism with a single KV cache manager embedded in a centralized scheduler, enabling efficient memory sharing across GPUs.

claim.statement: vLLM supports distributed execution of large language models by integrating Megatron-LM style tensor model parallelism with a single KV cache manager embedded in a centralized scheduler, enabling efficient memory sharing across GPUs.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: distributed execution of large language models

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical block -> physical block via centralized scheduler

children.statement: The single KV cache manager in vLLM shares a logical-to-physical block mapping across GPU workers, allowing each worker to store only the portion of the KV cache corresponding to its assigned attention heads.

claim.statement: The single KV cache manager in vLLM shares a logical-to-physical block mapping across GPU workers, allowing each worker to store only the portion of the KV cache corresponding to its assigned attention heads.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: reduces per-GPU KV cache memory usage

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV block -> physical KV block via shared mapping
systems_facets.s5_policy: partition KV cache per attention head

children.statement: The design of vLLM facilitates effective memory management for various decoding methods, such as parallel sampling, beam search, and shared prompt generation.

claim.statement: The design of vLLM facilitates effective memory management for various decoding methods, such as parallel sampling, beam search, and shared prompt generation.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: KV cache -> GPU memory pages via PagedAttention and KV cache manager

children.statement: Memory saving on parallel sampling ranges from 6.1% to 9.8% and on beam search ranges from 37.6% to 55.2% on the Alpaca dataset.
Memory saving on the ShareGPT dataset ranges from 16.2% to 30.5% on parallel sampling and from 44.3% to 66.3% on beam search.
The design of vLLM handles variable-length input and output sequences, allowing efficient KV cache usage across requests of differing lengths.

claim.statement: The design of vLLM handles variable-length input and output sequences, allowing efficient KV cache usage across requests of differing lengths.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Efficient KV cache usage vs. variable-length sequence handling

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV cache -> physical GPU memory via KV cache manager

children.statement: ShareGPT dataset has 8.4× longer input prompts and 5.8× longer outputs on average than the Alpaca dataset.
PagedAttention makes vLLM significantly outperform FasterTransformer in end-to-end performance.
vLLM is an end-to-end serving system with a FastAPI frontend and a GPU-based inference engine.

claim.statement: vLLM is an end-to-end serving system with a FastAPI frontend and a GPU-based inference engine.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: KV block -> GPU memory via block manager

children.statement: The frontend extends the OpenAI API interface, allowing users to customize sampling parameters for each request, such as maximum sequence length and beam width k.

claim.statement: The frontend extends the OpenAI API interface, allowing users to customize sampling parameters for each request, such as maximum sequence length and beam width k.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: KV block -> GPU memory via block manager

children.statement: The vLLM engine is written in 8.5K lines of Python and 2K lines of C++/CUDA code.

claim.statement: The vLLM engine is written in 8.5K lines of Python and 2K lines of C++/CUDA code.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: KV block -> GPU memory via block manager

children.statement: We develop control-related components including the scheduler and the block manager in Python while developing custom CUDA kernels for key operations such as PagedAttention.

claim.statement: We develop control-related components including the scheduler and the block manager in Python while developing custom CUDA kernels for key operations such as PagedAttention.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: UNSPECIFIED
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: NVIDIA GPU with CUDA and NCCL support

children.statement: In the Alpaca trace, block sizes 16 and 32 work well, while larger block sizes significantly degrade performance.
For the model executor, we implement popular LLMs such as GPT, OPT, and LLaMA using PyTorch and Transformers.

claim.statement: For the model executor, we implement popular LLMs such as GPT, OPT, and LLaMA using PyTorch and Transformers.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: UNSPECIFIED

children.statement: We use NCCL for tensor communication across distributed GPU workers.

claim.statement: We use NCCL for tensor communication across distributed GPU workers.

systems_facets.s1_resource: tensor communication across distributed GPU workers
systems_facets.s4_mapping: tensor -> GPU worker via NCCL

children.statement: Each request’s KV cache is represented as a series of logical KV blocks that are filled sequentially, with the last block’s unfilled positions reserved for future generations.

claim.statement: Each request’s KV cache is represented as a series of logical KV blocks that are filled sequentially, with the last block’s unfilled positions reserved for future generations.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: existing KV cache systems that pre-reserve memory for all positions

systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV blocks -> physical KV blocks via block tables
systems_facets.s5_policy: Allocate physical KV blocks on demand, reserving unfilled positions for future generations
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU with contiguous DRAM allocation capability

children.statement: The KV Cache Manager maintains block tables that map logical KV blocks to physical KV blocks, recording physical block numbers and the number of filled positions, allowing the cache to grow dynamically without pre‑reserving all positions.

claim.statement: The KV Cache Manager maintains block tables that map logical KV blocks to physical KV blocks, recording physical block numbers and the number of filled positions, allowing the cache to grow dynamically without pre‑reserving all positions.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV block -> physical KV block via block table mapping
systems_facets.s5_policy: Allocate physical KV blocks on demand as logical blocks are filled

children.statement: Separating logical and physical KV blocks eliminates most memory waste due to fragmentation and redundant duplication.

claim.statement: Separating logical and physical KV blocks eliminates most memory waste due to fragmentation and redundant duplication.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: Existing KV cache memory allocation methods that waste memory due to fragmentation and duplication

systems_facets.s4_mapping: logical KV block -> physical KV block via block table
systems_facets.s5_policy: allocate physical KV blocks on demand

children.statement: The serving system’s throughput is memory‑bound, constrained by the space allocated to store the KV cache.

claim.statement: The serving system’s throughput is memory‑bound, constrained by the space allocated to store the KV cache.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: KV cache -> GPU memory via allocation

children.statement: GPU computation speed grows faster than memory capacity; for example, from NVIDIA A100 to H100, FLOPS increases by more than 2× while GPU memory remains at 80 GB.
In our experiment (§6.3), the KV cache of the prompt part accounts for 12 % of the total KV cache memory.
In beam search, different request beams can share up to 55 % of their KV cache memory, as shown in §6.3.
Inefficient memory management can further decrease the batch size, as shown in Fig. 2.
The system needs to make scheduling decisions, such as deleting or swapping out the KV cache of some requests from GPU memory.
vLLM implements decoding algorithms using three key methods: fork, append, and free.

claim.statement: vLLM implements decoding algorithms using three key methods: fork, append, and free.

children.statement: The fork method creates a new sequence from an existing one.

claim.statement: The fork method creates a new sequence from an existing one.

children.statement: The append method appends a new token to the sequence.

claim.statement: The append method appends a new token to the sequence.

systems_facets.s4_mapping: UNSPECIFIED

negative_claims.statement: Inefficient memory management can further decrease the batch size, as shown in Fig. 2.
Compaction is impractical in performance-sensitive LLM serving systems due to the massive KV cache.
negative_claims.rejected_why: impractical due to massive KV cache

negative_claims.statement: Pre-allocated chunk space for each request prevents memory sharing specific to decoding algorithms in existing memory management systems.
negative_claims.rejected_why: prevents memory sharing specific to decoding algorithms
